questions.question: Explain the difference between linear recursion and binary recursion with examples.
keyPoints: Linear recursion: single recursive call (factorial, sum of array)
Binary recursion: two recursive calls (Fibonacci, merge sort, binary search)
Linear processes one subproblem at a time
Binary divides problem into two halves

questions.question: What is the base case in recursion? Why is it important?
keyPoints: Base case is the terminating condition
Prevents infinite recursion
Stops the chain of recursive calls
Without base case, stack overflow occurs

questions.question: Define tail recursion and explain its significance.
keyPoints: Tail recursion: recursive call is the last operation
Compiler can optimize to iterative form
Saves stack space
Examples: tail-recursive factorial

questions.question: What is memoization? How does it improve recursive algorithm performance?
keyPoints: Stores previously computed results in cache
Avoids redundant calculations
Changes time complexity from exponential to polynomial
Used in Fibonacci to achieve O(n) time

questions.question: Explain indirect recursion with an example.
keyPoints: When function A calls B and B calls A
Forms a circular calling pattern
Requires careful base case handling
Example: even(n) calls odd(n-1), odd(n) calls even(n-1)

questions.question: Convert the recursive factorial function to its iterative equivalent.
keyPoints: Use a loop instead of recursive calls
Maintain accumulator variable
No stack memory used
Achieves O(n) time and O(1) space

questions.question: What is stack overflow in the context of recursion? How can it be prevented?
keyPoints: Occurs when too many recursive calls exhaust stack memory
Caused by missing/infinite recursion
Prevention: proper base case, tail optimization, convert to iteration
Large input sizes increase risk

questions.question: Write a recursive algorithm to solve the Tower of Hanoi problem. Analyze its time complexity and explain why it represents tree recursion.
keyPoints: Algorithm: move n-1 disks to auxiliary, move largest to target, move n-1 to target
Time complexity: O(2^n) - exponential
Makes more than 2 recursive calls (tree-like branching)
For n disks, total moves = 2^n - 1
Demonstrates tree recursion pattern

questions.question: Compare and contrast linear recursion, binary recursion, and tree recursion. Provide examples of each and analyze their time complexities.
keyPoints: Linear: 1 call (factorial O(n), sum O(n))
Binary: 2 calls (Fibonacci naive O(2^n), optimized O(n), merge sort O(nlogn))
Tree: >2 calls (Tower of Hanoi O(2^n))
Discuss space complexity for each
Use cases and practical applications

questions.question: Write a recursive algorithm to calculate the nth Fibonacci number. Analyze its time and space complexity. Then propose optimizations to improve its performance.
keyPoints: Naive recursive: F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2), O(2^n) time, O(n) space
Memoization approach: O(n) time, O(n) space
Iterative approach: O(n) time, O(1) space
Explain memoization implementation
Discuss when to use each approach

questions.question: Explain recursion vs iteration. When should one be preferred over the other? Discuss with examples from data structures.
keyPoints: Recursion: elegant, natural for tree/graph problems, uses stack memory
Iteration: efficient memory usage, explicit control
Use recursion for: tree traversals, DFS, divide-and-conquer
Use iteration for: simple loops, memory-constrained environments
Trade-offs: readability vs efficiency
